title: The Jordaan
standfirst: Amsterdam's most beloved neighbourhood — narrow canals, 17th-century houses, hidden courtyards, and the kind of brown cafés where you lose track of an afternoon.
intro_html:
The Jordaan started as a mistake. When Amsterdam expanded its canal ring in the early 17th century, the city planners drew the new residential quarter to the west — narrow streets, dense housing, no grand waterways. It was built for workers: tanners, cloth-dyers, and craftsmen who couldn't afford the canal ring addresses being snapped up by Golden Age merchants. The name probably comes from the French jardin — garden — because the area was laid out on what had been market gardens.

It stayed working-class for three centuries. The Jordaan had a reputation for poverty, overcrowding, and radical politics well into the 20th century. Then, slowly, the artists arrived. Then the galleries. Then the boutiques. Today the Jordaan has some of the highest residential property prices in the Netherlands, and the canal houses that once housed ten people to a room are selling for two million euros. The narrow streets are still there. The brown cafés are still there. The hofjes — hidden almshouse courtyards behind unmarked doors — are still there. The Jordaan absorbed its own gentrification without quite losing its character. That's a rare thing.

This is the neighbourhood where Amsterdam feels most like itself: unhurried, slightly eccentric, genuinely beautiful. Give it at least a morning. Give it a full day if you can.

todos.title: Wander de 9 Straatjes (the Nine Streets)
todos.description: The nine streets cross the Jordaan's three main canals (Keizersgracht, Herengracht, Prinsengracht) and contain the best concentration of independent boutiques in Amsterdam — vintage clothing, concept stores, specialist bookshops, and design studios. Start at Reestraat and work your way south. Allow 2 hours minimum.

todos.title: Find the hidden hofjes
todos.description: The Jordaan contains at least 20 hidden courtyards (hofjes) — former almshouses tucked behind unmarked doors on ordinary-looking streets. The best are Karthuizershofje (Karthuizerstraat 87), Claes Claeszhofje (1e Egelantiersdwarsstraat 3), and Suykerhofje (Lindengracht 149). Push the door. Walk in quietly.

todos.title: Saturday Noordermarkt
todos.description: Every Saturday morning, the Noordermarktplein fills with two markets running simultaneously: the Boerenmarkt (organic farmers' market, 9am–4pm) and the Noordermarkt antique & vintage market (9am–2pm). This is where Jordaan residents actually shop. Arrive at 9am for the best selection and the fewest crowds.

todos.title: Sit in a brown café (bruin café)
todos.description: Amsterdam's brown cafés are the Dutch equivalent of a London pub — dark wood, candles in bottles, a regulars' bar that's been the same for decades. In the Jordaan, Café 't Smalle (Egelantiersgracht 12) and Café de Reiger (Nieuwe Leliestraat 34) are the most authentic. Order a jenever (Dutch gin) and a beer. Don't rush.

todos.title: Walk the Prinsengracht and Brouwersgracht
todos.description: The Prinsengracht is the outer ring of the main canal network and one of the most beautiful streets in the city — wide enough to feel airy, lined with elms, houseboats moored along both banks. Walk north from the Anne Frank House to the Brouwersgracht junction: this corner is consistently cited as the most beautiful canal intersection in Amsterdam.

todos.title: Explore the Westerkerk and Westermarkt
todos.description: The Westerkerk tower (1631) is the tallest church tower in Amsterdam and visible from most of the Jordaan. Rembrandt is buried somewhere inside — the exact location was lost in the 18th century. Every Monday morning, the Westermarkt hosts one of Amsterdam's longest-running outdoor markets. The Anne Frank House is directly beside it.

foods.title: Winkel 43
foods.description: The appeltaart at Winkel 43 (Noordermarkt 43) has been called the best apple pie in Amsterdam for 20 years running. It's served warm, with a thick wedge of whipped cream. Arrive before 11am on Saturdays to beat the Noordermarkt queue.

foods.title: Café 't Smalle
foods.description: One of the oldest brown cafés in Amsterdam, operating from a converted 18th-century jenever distillery on the Egelantiersgracht. Wooden benches extend over the canal in summer. Go on a weekday afternoon when it's quiet enough to hear the waterway.

foods.title: De Reiger
foods.description: A genuine neighbourhood restaurant on Nieuwe Leliestraat — good Dutch-French cooking, chalk-written menu, no reservations taken. Arrive before 7pm or expect to wait. The kind of place that's been full every night for thirty years without ever appearing in a travel guide.

foods.title: Lot Sixty One
foods.description: Amsterdam's best specialty coffee roaster, operating out of a converted garage on Kinkerstraat (five minutes west of the Jordaan). The espresso is exceptional. The beans are sourced directly from farms in Ethiopia, Colombia, and Guatemala. No wifi, on purpose.

products.title: Jordaan Walking Tour
products.description: A 2-hour small-group guided walk through the nine streets, hidden hofjes, and canal-side architecture with a local guide who actually lives here.

products.title: Anne Frank House
products.description: The Jordaan's most significant site — and the most visited in Amsterdam. Pre-booking is essential. Time slots release on the 1st of each month and sell out in hours.

products.title: Canal Cruise from Prinsengracht
products.description: Several operators depart from near the Anne Frank House — putting you in the most scenic stretch of the canal ring from the first minute.

products.title: Guided Bike Tour
products.description: The Jordaan is the starting point for most of Amsterdam's guided bike tours — flat streets, quiet canals, and the perfect pace for seeing the city on two wheels.

getting_there.text: Tram 13, 17 or 19 to Westermarkt stop — puts you at the northern Jordaan entrance, directly beside the Westerkerk and Anne Frank House.
20-minute walk from Amsterdam Centraal Station along the Singel canal, or 15 minutes from the Rijksmuseum area via Leidsegracht.
Most Amsterdam bike rental shops offer bikes from around €10/day. Cycling to the Jordaan from Centraal takes about 10 minutes.
Don't drive. Parking in the Jordaan costs €7.50/hour and spaces are rare. Tram or bike in every time.
